fact: September 12 is exactly 255 days into the year - that's the same as 2 to the 8th power in math!

In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when monarch butterflies are typically flying thousands of miles south, and some of them are great-great-grandchildren of the ones that made the trip north in spring.

Trees often start changing colors right around now in northern climates because they're getting ready for winter by pulling all the green chlorophyll out of their leaves, revealing hidden reds and yellows that were there all along!
The sun rises and sets almost exactly 12 hours apart around this date because we're only 10 days away from the autumn equinox when day and night are perfectly equal.
September 12 falls in the middle of Virgo season, making it one of the most 'Virgo-ish' days of the entire year for anyone who follows astrology.
Squirrels typically go into overdrive around this time - they can bury up to 25 nuts per day and remember where they put thousands of them using landmarks and smell.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 12 is when spring flowers like wattle trees often explode into bright yellow blooms that can be seen from miles away.
Morning dew usually gets super heavy around this date in many places because warm days and cool nights create perfect conditions for water to condense on grass and spider webs.
September 12 is when many birds that migrate at night use the stars to navigate, and they can actually see magnetic fields through special proteins in their eyes!
Apple trees typically hit their peak ripeness right around now in many northern regions, and a single apple tree can produce enough fruit to make 20 pies.
The moon often looks extra orange or red when it rises around this time of year because of dust and particles floating in the atmosphere from harvest season.
September 12 usually marks the time when many spiders finish building their biggest, most impressive webs of the year to catch flying insects before winter hits.
